previous_paragraph: In the novel "An Inspector Calls" Priestley presents the characters in different ways. Sheila is quite willing to take responsibility and so is Eric, Gerald is quite quiet, Mrs Birling refuses to take any responsibility. Then there's Mr Birling, who Priestley presents as a "hard-headed, practical man of business" and looking into that, it becomes clear Arthur Birling is selfish and rather self-centered at times aswell. In "An Inspector Calls" Priestley presents Arthur Birling as a hard-headed, practical man of business by using different techniques. "But take my word for it, you youngsters - and I've learnt in the good hard school of experience - that a man has to mind his own business and look after himself and his own -." The direct address "you youngsters" suggests a dominant tone in Mr Birling's voice which shows he is quite possessive and likes to make sure people are listening to him. The repetition of his, himself, his also emphasises the fact he is quite possessive. Priestley also uses asyndetic listing "his own business and look after himself, and his own" by doing this its like Birling is trying to suggest men have such a hard life with a lot of responsibilities and suggest he is sexist as men have to do everything. This shows the reader Birling is hard-headed as he is quite self-centered. Furthermore Priestley presents Mr Birling as a practical man of business and who has had a lot of experience. "Well, it's my duty to keep labour costs down, and if I'd agreed to this demand for a new rate we'd have added about twelve per cent to our labour costs." Here, this quote shows Mr Birling is a man of business because he uses statistics to explain to the inspector the consequences of taking on Eva Smith. The Plosive alliteration used in this sentence suggest Mr Birling becoming slightly aggitated over the fact the inspector was questioning his decision, suggesting Mr Birling didn't know what he was doing. The audience can see here that Mr Birling is a practical man of business as he puts the inspector back in his place showing that he knows how to run a business.
current_paragraph: Finally Priestley presents Mr Birling as self-centered and selfish. This is shown a few times throughout the novel. "I told the girl to clear out, and she went." and "Have you any idea what happened to her after that? Get into trouble? Go on the streets?" Mr Birling in his first quote is quite short and abrupt, this suggests he is trying to dismiss the matter quickly as it no longer concerns him. The use of questioning and sticatto in the second quote suggests he is bitter and trying to find any way he can to put the blame on someone else. This suggests to the Audience that unless it means something to Mr Birling or it earns him money, he's not interested in it.
